frame_label: Sindoor, Mangalsutra, Shankha-Pola, Name Change: Looking Married
generated_comment:
Spoken conversation:
A: I know it's a personal choice, but honestly a little sindoor or mangalsutra does give that settled, suhagan look.
B: Not wearing it doesn't change the marriage.
A: Of course, but some traditions are not about control yaar, they're part of our culture and they mean something.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames visible marital markers as making a woman look more 'settled' and culturally proper, while masking the pressure as respect for tradition and personal choice. It encodes completeness framing and disapproval of non-wearing through a progressive-sounding voice.
persona_text: You are Geeta. Age 29. Single. A woman working as a data scientist in Bengaluru. You are at your best friend's wedding. Your friend is leaving her job at a tech company to relocate with her husband who has been transferred to another city. Your friend seems happy. At the reception you are talking to an older relative of the groom who has just heard that you are not married and work in tech. The relative asks you when you plan to settle down. You say you are settled Ñ you have an apartment, a job you love, savings, a life you have built. The relative smiles and says no beti I mean when will you really settle down. You are looking at this relative and deciding what to say next.
